full_context: For the classification of hazardous areas with secondary release sources, the extent of the zone is limited by parameters such as dust quantity and release rate. The hazardous area radius is calculated by: R = 7.5 × ∛(Q/C), where Q is the release rate and C is the lower explosive limit concentration. This formula is applicable for determining area 21 (flammable dust environment).

variable_definitions.name: Hazardous area radius
variable_definitions.meaning: Radius of the hazardous area centered on the release source

variable_definitions.name: Release rate
variable_definitions.meaning: Quantity of flammable substance released per unit time

variable_definitions.name: Lower explosive limit concentration
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum concentration for explosive mixture formation by the substance

potential_questions: Given acetone release rate Q=0.05kg/s, lower explosive limit C=0.037kg/m³, calculate hazardous area radius R
task_description: 1. Obtain the release rate \(Q\). 2. Determine the lower explosive limit concentration \(C\) of the substance. 3. Compute the ratio \(Q/C\). 4. Calculate the cube root \(\sqrt[3]{Q/C}\). 5. Multiply by 7.5. 6. Output the radius \(R\).

full_context: Limit state design for load carrying capacity in buildings and structures should satisfy: γ₀S ≤ R. Here, γ₀ is the structural importance coefficient, S is the combined design value of load effects, R is the designed resistance value of the structural component. This formula is a basic principle for substation civil design, applicable to strength verification of all buildings and structures.

variable_definitions.name: Structural importance coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Coefficient reflecting the safety level of the structure, value specified by GB50068

variable_definitions.name: Combined load effect value
variable_definitions.meaning: Comprehensive effect value under combined loads

variable_definitions.name: Structural resistance design value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum effect withstandable by component

potential_questions: Given γ₀=1.0, S=850kN·m, R=1200kN·m for a 35kV structure, check if this satisfies the requirement
task_description: 1. Determine the structural importance coefficient \(\gamma_0\). 2. Compute the combined load effect \(S\). 3. Obtain the structural resistance design value \(R\). 4. Check if \(\gamma_0 S \le R\) is true. 5. Output the verification result.

full_context: Foundation anti-overturning verification formula: K_s = M_resist/M_overturn ≥ 1.8, where M_resist is the restoring moment of the foundation, and M_overturn is the overturning moment due to external forces. This formula is used to check the stability of equipment support under extreme wind load and is a mandatory requirement in substation civil design.

variable_definitions.name: Anti-overturning safety factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety margin for anti-overturning capability

variable_definitions.name: Restoring moment against overturning
variable_definitions.meaning: Moment resisting overturning due to self-weight, etc.

variable_definitions.name: Overturning moment
variable_definitions.meaning: Moment causing overturning due to wind load, etc.

potential_questions: Given equipment support foundation M_resist=6500kN·m, M_overturn=3200kN·m for 110kV substation, check overturning safety factor
task_description: 1. Calculate \(M_{\text{resist}}\) due to foundation self-weight. 2. Calculate \(M_{\text{overturn}}\) due to wind load. 3. Compute \(K_s=M_{\text{resist}}/M_{\text{overturn}}\). 4. Compare \(K_s\) with 1.8. 5. Output the verification conclusion.

full_context: The environmental temperature correction factor for the long-term allowable current-carrying capacity of conductors is calculated using: K_t = √[(t_c - t_a)/(t_c - t_n)]. This formula is for adjusting the ampacity when the actual ambient temperature differs from the reference. It is applicable to the calculation of current-carrying capacity for bare conductors indoors and outdoors.

variable_definitions.name: Temperature correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction factor for ampacity due to ambient temperature changes

variable_definitions.name: Maximum allowable conductor temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Highest working temperature conductor is allowed

variable_definitions.name: Actual ambient temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Measured temperature at conductor installation location

variable_definitions.name: Reference ambient temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Reference temperature for standard ampacity

potential_questions: Copper conductor: t_c=70℃, t_n=25℃, t_a=40℃, calculate the ampacity correction factor
task_description: 1. Determine the maximum allowable temperature \(t_c\) for conductor material. 2. Obtain the actual ambient temperature \(t_a\). 3. Find the reference temperature \(t_n\). 4. Compute \((t_c-t_a)/(t_c-t_n)\). 5. Take square root for \(K_t\). 6. Output \(K_t\).

full_context: The corona critical voltage for 110kV conductors must satisfy: U_critical > U_operating. This mandatory provision in high-voltage device design ensures no corona discharge at operating voltage, safeguarding equipment operation.

variable_definitions.name: Corona critical voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum voltage to initiate corona discharge

variable_definitions.name: Maximum operating voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Highest working voltage during normal operation

potential_questions: 110kV system U_operating=126kV, conductor U_critical=130kV, check specification compliance
task_description: 1. Identify system maximum operating voltage \(U_{\text{operating}}\). 2. Calculate or obtain conductor corona critical voltage \(U_{\text{critical}}\). 3. Check if \(U_{\text{critical}}>U_{\text{operating}}\) is satisfied. 4. Output verification result.

full_context: Battery capacity calculation formula: I_battery = (Q_emergency × T)/(V_system × η). Used for DC system design in hazardous environments; T is emergency discharge time, Q_emergency is emergency load power, η is discharge efficiency coefficient.

variable_definitions.name: Rated battery capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Capacity required to meet emergency power demand

variable_definitions.name: Emergency load capacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Total power required during emergency state

variable_definitions.name: Emergency discharge time
variable_definitions.meaning: Required supply duration

variable_definitions.name: DC system voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of battery group

variable_definitions.name: Discharge efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Efficiency coefficient considering aging etc.

potential_questions: Emergency load 15kW, discharge time 2h, system voltage 220V, η=0.85, calculate battery capacity
task_description: 1. Determine the emergency load \(Q_{\text{emergency}}\). 2. Identify required discharge time \(T\). 3. Obtain system voltage \(V_{\text{system}}\). 4. Select efficiency coefficient \(\eta\). 5. Compute \(I_{\text{battery}}=(Q_{\text{emergency}}\times T)/(V_{\text{system}}\times\eta)\). 6. Output required capacity.

full_context: Set the calibration value of the transmitter to I_bx=5A or 1A, then the full-scale value of the ammeter is calculated as: I_bl = I_le. This formula computes the ammeter’s full-scale value based on the primary rated current of the current transformer, applicable to design of measuring instruments in power installations.

variable_definitions.name: Ammeter full-scale value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum range of ammeter

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer primary rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Primary rated working current of CT

potential_questions: Current transformer primary rated current I_le=1000A, calculate ammeter full-scale value I_bl
task_description: 1. Obtain CT primary rated current \(I_{le}\). 2. Set \(I_{bl}=I_{le}\). 3. Output the full-scale ammeter value \(I_{bl}\).

full_context: When the transmitter is set to U_bx=100V, the voltmeter full-scale value is calculated as: U_bl = K × U_le. K is the input voltage multiple for the voltage transmitter (typically 1.2–1.5). This formula guides voltmeter range setting to match transmitter input.

variable_definitions.name: Voltmeter full-scale value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum range of voltmeter

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer primary rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Primary rated working voltage of VT

variable_definitions.name: Input voltage multiple
variable_definitions.meaning: Input range adjustment factor for transmitter

potential_questions: Voltage transformer primary rated voltage U_le=10kV, K=1.3, calculate voltmeter full-scale value U_bl
task_description: 1. Obtain VT primary rated voltage \(U_{le}\). 2. Determine input voltage multiple \(K\). 3. Calculate \(U_{bl}=K\times U_{le}\). 4. Output voltmeter full-scale value \(U_{bl}\).

full_context: The full-scale value of active power meter is: P_bl = √3 × U_le × I_le. The formula is based on VT primary rated voltage and CT primary rated current; it sets the range for active power meter used in defined-ampacity equipment.

variable_definitions.name: Active power meter full-scale value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum range of active power meter

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer primary rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Primary rated voltage of VT

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer primary rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Primary rated current of CT

potential_questions: U_le=10kV, I_le=100A, calculate active power meter full-scale value P_bl
task_description: 1. Obtain VT primary rated voltage \(U_{le}\). 2. Obtain CT primary rated current \(I_{le}\). 3. Calculate \(\sqrt{3}\) (≈1.732). 4. Compute \(P_{bl}=\sqrt{3}\times U_{le}\times I_{le}\). 5. Output active power meter full-scale value \(P_{bl}\).

full_context: The full-scale value of reactive power meter is: Q_bl = √3 × U_le × I_le. It sets the range for the reactive power meter based on VT primary rated voltage and CT primary rated current for specialized equipment.

variable_definitions.name: Reactive power meter full-scale value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum range of reactive power meter

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer primary rated voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Primary rated voltage of VT

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer primary rated current
variable_definitions.meaning: Primary rated current of CT

potential_questions: U_le=10kV, I_le=100A, calculate reactive power meter full-scale value Q_bl
task_description: 1. Obtain VT primary rated voltage \(U_{le}\). 2. Obtain CT primary rated current \(I_{le}\). 3. Calculate \(\sqrt{3}\) (≈1.732). 4. Compute \(Q_{bl}=\sqrt{3}\times U_{le}\times I_{le}\). 5. Output reactive power meter full-scale value \(Q_{bl}\).

full_context: The active energy meter is calculated as: W_P1 = W_P2 × (N_u × N_i). This formula converts the secondary-side meter reading to the primary-side actual energy value, using voltage transformer and current transformer ratios.

variable_definitions.name: Primary active energy value
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual active energy consumed by the primary power system

variable_definitions.name: Secondary active energy meter reading
variable_definitions.meaning: Active energy indicated by secondary-side meter

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of VT primary to secondary voltage

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of CT primary to secondary current

potential_questions: Energy meter reading W_P2=500kW·h, N_u=100, N_i=20, calculate actual primary energy W_P1
task_description: 1. Read secondary-side active energy value \(W_{P2}\). 2. Obtain voltage transformer ratio \(N_u\). 3. Obtain current transformer ratio \(N_i\). 4. Calculate ratio product \(N_u\times N_i\). 5. Compute \(W_{P1}=W_{P2}\times(N_u\times N_i)\). 6. Output actual primary energy \(W_{P1}\).

full_context: Current transmitter calibration value is: I_bx = I_b3 / N_i. The formula determines calibration value using instrument full-scale current and CT ratio.

variable_definitions.name: Current transmitter calibration value
variable_definitions.meaning: Output current calibration value for transmitter

variable_definitions.name: Instrument full-scale current value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum secondary instrument current range

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of CT primary to secondary current

potential_questions: Instrument full-scale current I_b3=5A, N_i=100, calculate transmitter calibration value I_bx
task_description: 1. Obtain instrument full-scale current value \(I_{b3}\). 2. Obtain CT ratio \(N_i\). 3. Compute \(I_{bx}=I_{b3}/N_i\). 4. Output transmitter calibration value \(I_{bx}\).

full_context: Voltage transmitter calibration value is: U_bx = U_bl / N_u. The formula determines calibration value using instrument full-scale voltage and VT ratio.

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transmitter calibration value
variable_definitions.meaning: Output voltage calibration value for transmitter

variable_definitions.name: Instrument full-scale voltage value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum secondary instrument voltage range

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of VT primary to secondary voltage

potential_questions: Instrument full-scale voltage U_bl=100V, N_u=1000, calculate transmitter calibration value U_bx
task_description: 1. Obtain instrument full-scale voltage value \(U_{bl}\). 2. Obtain VT ratio \(N_u\). 3. Compute \(U_{bx}=U_{bl}/N_u\). 4. Output transmitter calibration value \(U_{bx}\).

full_context: Active power transmitter calibration value: P_bx = P_bl / (N_u × N_i). The formula uses instrument full-scale value and VT, CT ratios.

variable_definitions.name: Active power transmitter calibration value
variable_definitions.meaning: Output active power calibration value for transmitter

variable_definitions.name: Instrument full-scale active power value
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum secondary instrument range for active power

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: VT primary to secondary voltage ratio

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: CT primary to secondary current ratio

potential_questions: Instrument full-scale active power P_bl=866W, N_u=100, N_i=20, calculate transmitter calibration value P_bx
task_description: 1. Obtain instrument full-scale active power value \(P_{bl}\). 2. Obtain VT ratio \(N_u\). 3. Obtain CT ratio \(N_i\). 4. Calculate ratio product \(N_u\times N_i\). 5. Compute \(P_{bx}=P_{bl}/(N_u\times N_i)\). 6. Output active power transmitter calibration value \(P_{bx}\).

full_context: The primary active energy value is: W_P1 = A × (N_u × N_i) / C. This formula converts total pulse count and meter constant to actual primary energy, using VT and CT ratios.

variable_definitions.name: Primary active energy value
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual active energy consumption of the primary power system

variable_definitions.name: Cumulative pulse count
variable_definitions.meaning: Total pulse count output by energy meter

variable_definitions.name: Voltage transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: VT primary to secondary voltage ratio

variable_definitions.name: Current transformer ratio
variable_definitions.meaning: CT primary to secondary current ratio

variable_definitions.name: Energy meter constant
variable_definitions.meaning: Number of pulses per kW·h

potential_questions: Pulse count A=5000, N_u=100, N_i=20, energy meter constant C=1000 pulses/kW·h, calculate primary energy value W_P1
task_description: 1. Read cumulative pulse count \(A\) from energy meter. 2. Obtain VT ratio \(N_u\). 3. Obtain CT ratio \(N_i\). 4. Calculate ratio product \(N_u\times N_i\). 5. Obtain energy meter constant \(C\). 6. Compute \(W_{P1}=A\times(N_u\times N_i)/C\). 7. Output primary active energy value \(W_{P1}\).

full_context: The grounding resistance for effective and low-resistance grounding systems must satisfy: R ≤ 2000 / I_G, where R is the greatest grounding resistance considering seasonal variation (Ω), and I_G is the maximum asymmetric fault current entering ground via grid (A). This ensures the grid limits rise in ground potential during faults.

variable_definitions.name: Grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum grounding resistance considering seasonal change

variable_definitions.name: Maximum asymmetric fault current
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum ground fault current (including DC) during system annual peak year

potential_questions: 500kV substation: I_G=12.26kA, calculate maximum allowable grid grounding resistance
task_description: 1. Obtain \(I_G\) (12.26 kA). 2. Apply formula \(R\le 2000/I_G\). 3. Compute \(R\le 2000/12260\approx 0.163\,\Omega\). 4. Output the result.

full_context: The allowable touch voltage difference is calculated to ensure personnel safety during ground faults. ρ_s is surface resistivity (Ω·m); C_s is surface reduction coefficient; t_s is duration of ground fault current (s). Applicable to systems with 110kV and above effective grounding.

variable_definitions.name: Allowable touch voltage difference
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum allowable touch voltage

variable_definitions.name: Surface layer resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Resistivity of surface soil

variable_definitions.name: Surface reduction coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction coefficient for surface resistivity effect

variable_definitions.name: Duration of fault current
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration of ground fault current

potential_questions: Given ρ_s=200Ω·m, C_s=0.8, t_s=0.4s, calculate maximum allowable touch voltage difference
task_description: 1. Obtain values for \(\rho_s\), \(C_s\), \(t_s\). 2. Compute numerator \(174+0.17\times 200\times 0.8=174+27.2=201.2\). 3. Calculate denominator \(\sqrt{0.4}\approx 0.632\). 4. Compute \(U_t=201.2/0.632\approx 318\,\mathrm{V}\). 5. Output the result.

full_context: Lightning impulse grounding resistance formula computes equivalent grounding resistance for tower grounding under lightning current. R is power-frequency grounding resistance (Ω), α is impulse coefficient (depends on soil resistivity and lightning peak current).

variable_definitions.name: Lightning impulse grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent grounding resistance under lightning current

variable_definitions.name: Power-frequency grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Grounding resistance under power-frequency current

variable_definitions.name: Impulse coefficient
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction coefficient considering lightning impulse effect

potential_questions: Tower: R=15Ω, soil resistivity ρ=500Ω·m, α=0.7, calculate impulse grounding resistance
task_description: 1. Obtain \(R\) value (15 Ω). 2. Determine \(\alpha\) value (0.7). 3. Compute \(R_i=\alpha\times R=0.7\times 15=10.5\,\Omega\). 4. Output result.

full_context: Line insulator string operating impulse strength requirement: U_{l,is} is the positive polarity 50% discharge voltage, U_s is statistical operating overvoltage, k_1 is margin factor (1.27). Ensures reliable operation under switching surges.

variable_definitions.name: Operating impulse 50% discharge voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Voltage at 50% probability discharge for insulator string under impulse

variable_definitions.name: Statistical operating overvoltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Statistical value of system switching overvoltage

variable_definitions.name: Coordination factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Safety margin factor

potential_questions: 500kV line: U_s=1000kV, calculate minimum required U_{l,is}
task_description: 1. Obtain \(U_s\) (1000 kV). 2. Use \(k_1=1.27\). 3. Compute \(U_{l,is}\ge k_1\times U_s = 1.27\times 1000=1270\,\mathrm{kV}\). 4. Output result.

full_context: Grounding resistance calculation for vertical ground rod in uniform soil at power frequency, applicable where ground rod length is much greater than diameter. ρ is soil resistivity (Ω·m), l is ground rod length (m), d is ground rod diameter (m).

variable_definitions.name: Vertical ground rod grounding resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Power frequency grounding resistance of vertical ground electrode

variable_definitions.name: Soil resistivity
variable_definitions.meaning: Electrical property of soil for conductivity

variable_definitions.name: Ground rod length
variable_definitions.meaning: Length of vertical ground electrode

variable_definitions.name: Ground rod diameter
variable_definitions.meaning: Equivalent diameter of ground electrode

potential_questions: Calculate power-frequency grounding resistance of a 3m long, 0.02m diameter steel ground rod in ρ=100Ω·m soil
task_description: 1. Obtain \(\rho=100\,\Omega\cdot\mathrm{m}\), \(l=3\,\mathrm{m}\), \(d=0.02\,\mathrm{m}\). 2. Compute \(\ln(8\times 3/0.02)=\ln(1200)\approx 7.09\). 3. Compute bracket \(7.09-1=6.09\). 4. Compute \(R_v=\frac{\rho}{2\pi l}\left(\ln\frac{8l}{d}-1\right)\approx \frac{100}{2\times 3.14\times 3}\times 6.09\approx 32.3\,\Omega\). 5. Output result.

full_context: The required number of detectors in one detection zone should not be less than N = S/(K·A), where N is detector count (rounded up), S is detection area (m²); K is correction factor (depends on max occupancy), A is protected area per detector. Used for calculating fire detector quantity.

variable_definitions.name: Number of detectors
variable_definitions.meaning: Required number of detectors in detection area

variable_definitions.name: Detection area
variable_definitions.meaning: Area covered by detectors

variable_definitions.name: Correction factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Correction coefficient based on occupancy

variable_definitions.name: Protected area
variable_definitions.meaning: Area effectively detected by one detector

potential_questions: Detection area S=600m², occupancy 1500, detector protected area A=60m², correction factor K=0.95; calculate required N.
task_description: 1. Determine correction factor \(K\) according to occupancy. 2. Determine detector protected area \(A\). 3. Calculate \(N=S/(K\cdot A)\). 4. Round up result to nearest integer.

full_context: For AC systems, the normal induced voltage at any non-directly grounded point on the cable metal sheath is: E_s = L · E_{s0}. L is the distance (km) from the point on the metal sheath to the direct grounding point, E_{s0} is the normal induced voltage per unit length (V/km). This formula calculates induced voltage on cable sheaths.

variable_definitions.name: Induced voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Induced voltage at a point on cable sheath

variable_definitions.name: Distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Distance from cable sheath point to direct grounding point

variable_definitions.name: Normal induced voltage per unit length
variable_definitions.meaning: Induced voltage per unit length of cable sheath

potential_questions: At a cable sheath point, L=2km, E_{s0}=50V/km, calculate normal induced voltage E_s.
task_description: 1. Determine distance \(L\). 2. Determine normal induced voltage per length \(E_{s0}\). 3. Compute \(E_s=L\times E_{s0}\).

full_context: Minimum allowable cable conductor cross-section under short-circuit thermal stability: S_{min} = I / C × √t; S_{min} is minimum conductor cross-section (mm²); I is effective short circuit current (A); t is short circuit duration (s); C is thermal stability factor (depends on material and temperature). It verifies cable thermal stability under short circuit.

variable_definitions.name: Minimum allowed cross-section
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum conductor cross-section meeting short-circuit thermal stability

variable_definitions.name: Effective short-circuit current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS of short-circuit current

variable_definitions.name: Short-circuit duration
variable_definitions.meaning: Duration of short-circuit current

variable_definitions.name: Thermal stability factor
variable_definitions.meaning: Factor determined by conductor material and working temperature

potential_questions: Short-circuit current I=25kA, duration t=0.5s, copper C=143, calculate minimum conductor cross-section S_{min}.
task_description: 1. Determine effective short-circuit current \(I\). 2. Determine duration \(t\). 3. Obtain thermal stability factor \(C\). 4. Compute \(S_{\min}=(I/C)\times\sqrt{t}\). 5. Output result.

full_context: For fire-fighting equipment requiring automatic fire alarm system linkage, the linkage trigger signal should be the logical AND of two independent alarm signals: linkage trigger signal = alarm signal 1 ∩ alarm signal 2. The formula means both independent alarm signals need to be true for system reliability.

variable_definitions.name: linkage trigger signal
variable_definitions.meaning: Boolean logic signal triggering fire-fighting device linkage

variable_definitions.name: First alarm signal
variable_definitions.meaning: Signal from the first independent alarm device

variable_definitions.name: Second alarm signal
variable_definitions.meaning: Signal from the second independent alarm device

potential_questions: If smoke detector alarm and manual call point signal are both true, what is the linkage trigger signal?
task_description: 1. Obtain alarm signal from first device \(\text{alarm signal}_1\). 2. Obtain alarm signal from second device \(\text{alarm signal}_2\). 3. Perform logical AND operation \(\text{alarm signal}_1\cap\text{alarm signal}_2\). 4. Output linkage trigger signal result.

formula: S_{PE} = \begin{cases} S & \text{if } S \leq 16 \\ 16 & \text{if } 16 < S \leq 35 \\ S/2 & \text{if } 35 < S \leq 400 \\ 200 & \text{if } 400 < S \leq 800 \\ S/4 & \text{if } S > 800 \end{cases}
full_context: The minimum section for protective earth conductor is determined by piecewise function: If phase conductor section S≤16mm², S_PE=S; 16<S≤35mm², S_PE=16mm²; 35<S≤400mm², S_PE=S/2; 400<S≤800mm², S_PE=200mm²; S>800mm², S_PE=S/4. The formula determines minimum cross-section of protective conductor.

variable_definitions.name: Protective earth conductor section
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum allowed section for PE conductor

variable_definitions.name: Phase conductor section
variable_definitions.meaning: Sectional area of cable phase conductor

potential_questions: Given phase conductor section S=50mm², calculate minimum S_PE.
task_description: 1. Identify phase conductor section \(S\). 2. Use \(S\) to select corresponding segment of piecewise function. 3. Compute \(S_{PE}\). 4. Output result.

full_context: The distance d from the lower surface of point-type smoke detector to ceiling should be: h≤6m, d=30–200mm; 6<h≤8m, d=70–250mm; 8<h≤10m, d=100–300mm; 10<h≤12m, d=150–350mm. The formula sets permitted ranges for detector distance to ceiling at different installation heights.

variable_definitions.name: Detector distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Vertical distance from detector lower surface to ceiling

variable_definitions.name: Installation height
variable_definitions.meaning: Height from floor to detector installation point

potential_questions: If installation height h=7m, what is the permitted detector-to-ceiling distance d?
task_description: 1. Determine installation height \(h\). 2. Select corresponding piecewise function segment based on \(h\). 3. Determine allowed range for \(d\). 4. Output range.

full_context: For AC single-core power cable lines, the maximum normal induced voltage must be: E_s ≤50V if no effective safety measures to prevent access to metal sheath; otherwise, E_s ≤300V. The formula stipulates limits according to implemented safety measures.

variable_definitions.name: Normal induced voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Power-frequency induced voltage on cable sheath

potential_questions: The measured E_s on a cable line is 280V and safety measures are in place. Does this comply with the specification?
task_description: 1. Measure induced voltage \(E_s\) on cable sheath. 2. Confirm presence of safety measures. 3. Compare \(E_s\) with corresponding limit. 4. Output verification result.

full_context: For a fire alarm control panel, total number and address count of connected fire detectors, manual call points, and modules should not exceed 3200; total address count for linkage control panel or linkage-type alarm panel should not exceed 1600. Formula specifies device capacity limits.

variable_definitions.name: Maximum device count
variable_definitions.meaning: Max number of device addresses supported by one fire alarm panel

variable_definitions.name: Maximum linkage point count
variable_definitions.meaning: Max number of module addresses supported by one linkage control panel

potential_questions: A project needs to connect 3500 detector points. Can a single fire alarm panel meet the requirement?
task_description: 1. Count total system device points \(N\). 2. Compare \(N\) with \(N_{\max}=3200\) limit. 3. Output verification result.

full_context: If cable conductor working temperature exceeds 70℃, calculation for continuous allowed ampacity should consider the effect of temperature rise: θ_max = θ_0 + (θ_H - θ_0) * (I_p/I_H)^2. This formula calculates conductor temperature under actual working current.

variable_definitions.name: Conductor working temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Maximum temperature conductor may reach during operation

variable_definitions.name: Ambient temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Temperature of environment surrounding cable

variable_definitions.name: Allowed maximum temperature
variable_definitions.meaning: Cable’s maximum allowed working temperature

variable_definitions.name: Actual working current
variable_definitions.meaning: Actual working current in cable

variable_definitions.name: Rated ampacity
variable_definitions.meaning: Allowed ampacity under standard conditions

potential_questions: Ambient temperature θ_0=40°C, allowed θ_H=90°C, I_H=200A, I_p=180A; calculate conductor working temperature θ_max.
task_description: 1. Determine ambient temperature \(\theta_0\). 2. Determine maximum allowed temperature \(\theta_H\). 3. Determine actual working current \(I_p\). 4. Determine rated ampacity \(I_H\). 5. Compute \(\theta_{\max}=\theta_0+(\theta_H-\theta_0)\times(I_p/I_H)^2\). 6. Output result.

full_context: Emergency power output for fire-fighting equipment should exceed 120% of full-load power of fire alarm and linkage system. Battery group capacity should guarantee operation for not less than 3h at full fire load. Formula states emergency power requirements.

variable_definitions.name: Emergency power output
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated output power of emergency source

variable_definitions.name: System total power
variable_definitions.meaning: Full-load power of fire alarm system

variable_definitions.name: Backup time
variable_definitions.meaning: Continuous power supply time provided by battery

potential_questions: Given system total power P_system=5kW, compute minimum emergency power P_backup and backup time T_backup.
task_description: 1. Determine system total power \(P_{\text{system}}\). 2. Calculate \(P_{\text{backup}}\ge 1.2\times P_{\text{system}}\). 3. Confirm \(T_{\text{backup}}\ge 3\,\mathrm{h}\). 4. Output result.
